text: The **Berthouville Treasure** is a collection of approximately ninety gilt-silver objects, including vessels like bowls, cups, pitchers, and plates, as well as two statuettes of the Gallo-Roman god Mercury. Dating from the 1st to 3rd centuries AD, the treasure was intentionally buried in antiquity and discovered in 1830 near Berthouville, France. Notable pieces include a repoussé silver jug made in Asia Minor and inscribed votive offerings. Weighing around 55 pounds (25 kg), the treasure primarily consists of ornate silverware, many bearing dedicatory inscriptions, showcasing ancient Roman craftsmanship and religious practices.

According to the 2000 census, Aire-la-Ville had 25,992 Roman Catholics (80.2% of the population), while 2,255 (7.0%) belonged to the Swiss Reformed Church. There were 464 members of an Orthodox church (1.43%), and 497 individuals (1.53%) belonged to another Christian church. The Jewish community comprised 5 individuals (0.02%), while 985 (3.04%) were Islamic. Additionally, 41 were Buddhist, and 82 were Hindu. About 3.74% of the population (1,212 individuals) belonged to no church, identified as agnostic or atheist, while 1,109 (3.42%) did not specify their religion. [17]

Manny Lagos, born in St. Paul, Minnesota, played college soccer for the NCAA Division-I Milwaukee Panthers from 1990 to 1992, earning NSCAA First-Team All-American honors in 1991. During and after college, he played for the Minnesota Thunder from 1990 to 1996 under the coaching of his father, Buzz Lagos. Alongside his time with the Thunder, Lagos had short stints abroad, playing for Clermont Foot in France's Third Division and Lleida in Spain's lower divisions, further honing his skills as a midfielder.

Sky News and BBC News reported that the East London Mosque on Whitechapel Road in Whitechapel was surrounded by armed police, with residents instructed to stay indoors. The mosque was evacuated at around 3pm, searched by officers, and police later confirmed it was a bomb scare, giving the all-clear shortly before 5pm.

The simplest method of land reclamation is known as "infilling," which involves filling an area with heavy rocks or cement, followed by clay, dirt, or soil, until the desired height is achieved. The material used in this process is referred to as "infill." For agricultural purposes, land is often reclaimed by draining submerged wetlands. In cases where displaced material may be contaminated, deep cement mixing is employed to contain the contaminants. Another method, land dredging, involves removing sediments and debris from the bottom of a body of water. This technique is not only used for creating new land but also for maintaining reclaimed land masses, as sedimentation naturally fills channels and harbors over time. Each method is chosen based on factors such as soil type, topography, and the intended use of the reclaimed land.

Poaceae, or grasses, are the most economically important plant family to humans. Their importance stems from their role as a food source for humans and livestock, with cereals like wheat, rice, and corn being the most vital. Grasses have been grown as food for domesticated animals for thousands of years. They are also used to manufacture cooking oils, fuels, cosmetics, and alcohols. Furthermore, grasses provide construction materials, furniture, and utensils, and are cultivated as turf for lawns and recreational areas.

The North West Shelf Venture, with over $25 billion invested since the early 1980s, is Australia's largest resource development and was the world's largest engineering project during its construction. Underpinned by vast gas reserves in the Carnarvon Basin, with an estimated total of 33 Tcf, it remains a cornerstone of Australian energy production and a significant contributor to global energy markets.

**Description:** *Hibbertia pustulata* is a low-growing shrub, typically reaching a modest height. Its foliage consists of linear to linear-lanceolate leaves, measuring (4.3–)6.0–7.5(–8.6) mm long and (0.6–)0.7–0.8(–1.0) mm wide, with acute tips, a cuneate base, and scattered coarse hairs, particularly along the margins and midribs. The leaves are sessile or nearly so, lacking a distinct petiole. The flowers are arranged in compact, axillary, or terminal racemes, each subtended by a small triangular bract (~0.7–1.1 mm long) with short cilia. The sepals are glabrous or sparsely ciliate, narrowly elliptic-lanceolate, and measure (5.5–)5.9–6.3 mm long. The petals are bright yellow, often with a crimson base, and the stamens are numerous, surrounding the carpels. Flowering occurs primarily in mid to late spring, extending into early summer, with blooms sometimes persisting into late autumn. The plant's inflorescences and foliage are often densely pubescent, adding to its distinctive appearance.

After the Second World War, No. 617 Squadron replaced its Avro Lancaster bombers with Avro Lincolns, later followed by the English Electric Canberra jet bomber in 1952. In 1955, the squadron was deployed to Malaya for four months before returning to RAF Binbrook, where it was disbanded on 15 December 1955. The squadron reformed at RAF Scampton on 1 May 1958 as part of RAF Bomber Command's V-bomber force, maintaining the UK's strategic nuclear deterrent. From August 1960, the squadron was equipped with the Avro Vulcan B1, and by 23 May 1961, its aircraft were upgraded to the Vulcan B1A, fitted with an electronic countermeasures (ECM) tailpod. The squadron's assigned role was high-level strategic bombing with a variety of free-fall nuclear bombs. Both the B1 and B1A types were equipped with various free-fall nuclear weapons, and American bombs were also supplied to the RAF V-bombers for a short period under the Project E arrangements.

According to the 2017 Census, Mirpur's population was approximately 456,000. Its original population comprises ethnic Paharis, with migration from Rajouri and Poonch districts beginning in 1947. The Mirpuri diaspora, exceeding 1 million globally, primarily resides in the UK, with significant communities in Pakistan, the Middle East, and North America.

Johann Lamont launched Labour's 2012 local election campaign in Edinburgh on 17 April 2012, setting out policies focused on creating jobs, improving education and childcare, and expanding training opportunities. She accused the SNP government of passing on 89% of UK spending cuts to Scottish councils and compared the SNP's management of councils to "putting Craig Whyte in charge of your tax return." A key objective for Labour was retaining control of Glasgow City Council amid a strong SNP challenge. After the elections, political academic John Curtice, writing for *The Guardian*, observed that Lamont "has undoubtedly done enough to dispel doubts about whether she was the right woman for the job." Lamont urged voters to prioritize local services over independence debates, warning that SNP gains would be a "step towards separation." (150 words)

"L'Autre Finistère" debuted at number 27 on the French chart on 27 February 1993, marking the highest debut position for that week. Two weeks later, it dropped to number 38 but eventually peaked at number 10 during the week of 20 March 1993. The song remained in the top 50 for 18 weeks, including 6 weeks in the top 20. On the European Hot 100, it debuted at number 89 on 6 March 1993 and peaked at number 47 during the week of 20 March 1993, staying on the chart for a total of 8 weeks.

On 29 May 2012, the bodies of 13 unidentified men were discovered in the Assukar area, approximately 50 kilometers (31 miles) east of Deir ez-Zor in eastern Syria. According to opposition activists, the victims were electrical workers who had refused to end a strike in protest of ongoing massacres. A United Nations report described the men as having their hands tied behind their backs, with some appearing to have been shot in the head at close range. The head of the U.N. observer mission in Syria, Major General Robert Mood, condemned the killings as an "appalling and inexcusable act" and called for restraint from all sides to end the cycle of violence. On 5 June, a militant group claimed responsibility for the killings, which had been widely condemned by international observers. The incident highlighted the escalating brutality of the Syrian civil war and the targeting of civilians and workers in the conflict.

The 1960 Kerala Legislative Assembly election saw the participation of four national parties: the Indian National Congress (INC), Communist Party of India (CPI), Praja Socialist Party (PSP), and Indian Union Muslim League (IUML), along with the state party Kerala Congress. The INC, PSP, and IUML formed a pre-poll alliance to counter the CPI, fielding 102 candidates collectively and supporting 11 independents. The CPI contested 97 seats, supporting five independents who joined its legislature party after the election.

In early 2014, Daniel Layus announced that Augustana had signed with Washington Square Music and would release a new album, *Life Imitating Life*, on April 22, 2014. Layus, now the sole member, premiered the lead single "Ash and Ember." This marked a new chapter for Augustana after parting ways with Epic Records and most original members in 2011, with Layus continuing to tour and record under the band's name.

As President of Wilmington University, Jack Varsalona received several awards recognizing his leadership and community involvement. While no award specifically for Italian-American community leaders is mentioned in the provided context, Varsalona was named Goodwill of Delaware and Delaware County's Community Leadership Advocate of the Year in 2024. This honor recognized Wilmington University's scholarships awarded to Goodwill program graduates and employees. The Rev. Roberto Balducelli Appreciation Award was intended for those with an unyielding commitment to their community.

To promote "Ljubav je" as the Bosnian Eurovision entry, Dalal Midhat-Talakic and Fuad Backovic Dean made several appearances across Europe. They performed on 1 March 2016 during the celebration of the Independence Day of Bosnia and Herzegovina at the Sarajevo City Hall. Additionally, they were invited to perform at the Clarion Hotel in Stockholm, Sweden, by the Bosnian diaspora. On 4 March, they performed during the presentation show of the 2016 Montenegrin Eurovision entry, held at the Hall Deen. The Bosnian representatives also performed at the Eurovision in Concert event on 9 April 2016, which took place at the AFAS Live venue in Amsterdam, hosted by the Melkweg. Furthermore, on 26 April, Dalal, Deen, and Jala appeared in a special Eurovision-themed edition of the RTV Pink programme Ami G Show in Serbia.

The core structural unit in many natural products, including azafluorenones, is the fluorenone framework, which has garnered significant research interest. Representative compounds like onychine, polyfothine, and isoursuline exhibit anti-malarial effects against *Plasmodium falciparum*. Cyathocaline interacts with DNA, while azafluorenone derivatives demonstrate antimalarial, antimicrobial, and anticancer activities. Additionally, they are used in fluorescence-based technologies and exhibit specific enzyme inhibition, highlighting their diverse biological and technological applications.

The **Cola Debrot Prize**, instituted by Curaçao's government in 1968 and named after poet Cola Debrot, is awarded annually for outstanding contributions in arts, science, and culture across rotating categories.

The New York Times Building was designed by Renzo Piano, marking his first architectural project in New York City. Developed through a partnership between The New York Times Company, Forest City Ratner Companies, and ING Real Estate, the building features interiors designed by Gensler. The main contractor for the core and shell was AMEC Construction Management, Inc., while the contractor for the Times space was Tishman Realty & Construction. Thornton-Tomasetti Group Inc. served as the structural engineer, with the wind consultancy provided by RWDI. The sealant was supplied by Tremco, and the steel was provided by the American Bridge Company. Flack + Kurtz designed the mechanical, electrical, and plumbing systems, and the site is officially owned by The New York Times Company.

The Khmer Loves Khmer Party combines elements from across the political spectrum, aiming to unite all Cambodians, both within the country and abroad, to foster peace, stability, social equality, and economic prosperity. The party seeks positive changes to advance freedom, equality, and national integrity. An example of the Khmer Loves Khmer Party promoting an idea on the right of the political spectrum is not given in the provided context. The Khmer Loves Khmer Party chooses to respect the Constitution of Cambodia, intending to protect it and prevent any actions that might undermine it, which seems to be part of the Khmer Loves Khmer Party's ideology.

Lucie Papineau, born in Quebec, Canada, earned a bachelor's degree in communications from the Université du Québec à Montréal. She wrote for *Petit Devoir*, a children's section of *Le Devoir*, from 1986 to 1988. Since 1998, she has been a regular contributor to *Magazine Enfants Québec*, writing a parenting column. Papineau is also an editor and author at the publisher Dominique et compagnie, where she has created numerous acclaimed children's books.

Walter Gilbert Alexander II was born in 1922 in Petersburg, Virginia. He came from a family of doctors who migrated from Virginia to New Jersey. Alexander attended Orange High School in New Jersey before receiving a scholarship to Rutgers University. He was a member of ROTC, the engineering program, and the Rutgers track team. A Tuskegee Airman, he graduated from Rutgers with a degree in mechanical engineering and subsequently worked for Douglas Aircraft as a draftsman in California.

Samvel Mkrtchyan translated over 30 books into Armenian, including Shakespeare's works, Faulkner, Eliot, and Joyce. He was the editor of *Artasahmanyan Grakanutyun*, a quarterly journal for translated literature.

**Qemah** (foaled 2013) is a bay Irish-bred Thoroughbred mare bred by Ecurie Cadran, SCEA Bissons & SAS I.E.I. She was offered as a yearling at the Arqana August Sale, where she was bought for €120,000 by Blandford Bloodstock on behalf of Al Shaqab Racing. Trained in France by Jean-Claude Rouget, she was primarily ridden by jockey Gregory Benoist during her racing career.

The Royal Botanic Garden, Sydney, spans a total area of 29 hectares (72 acres) and is surrounded by 51 hectares (126 acres) of open parklands known as The Domain. This garden forms a large natural amphitheatre that slopes down towards the picturesque Farm Cove. It is divided into four major precincts: the Lower Gardens, the Middle Gardens, the Palace Gardens, and the Bennelong precinct. Located approximately in the middle of these four precincts is the Palm Grove Centre, which offers a variety of facilities including a restaurant, café, visitors centre, and bookshop. The Royal Botanic Garden is renowned for its extensive collection of plants, featuring over 27,000 species from around the world, making it one of Sydney's premier attractions for both tourism and leisure.

Before her murder, Du'a Khalil Aswad was reportedly sheltered in Bashika by either a Yazidi tribal leader or a local Muslim Sheikh due to fears for her safety after her relationship with a Sunni Muslim boy and an overnight absence from home. Her family allegedly persuaded her she had been forgiven, prompting her return, though it remains unclear if they were involved in her subsequent killing. The ambush, recorded on video, involved an estimated eight or nine attackers, though the exact circumstances remain unclear. The footage does not confirm whether the same family members who convinced her to return participated in her stoning.

In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, Switzerland became a popular destination for winter vacations, attracting visitors from across Europe. Winter sports such as skiing, sledding, and bandy were played in towns like Geneva and St. Moritz. Students from Oxford and Cambridge traveled to Switzerland to compete in bandy, with their first match taking place in St. Moritz in 1885, serving as a predecessor to the annual Ice Hockey Varsity Match. The European Championship was held in Switzerland in 1913, likely due to the popularity of its winter sport venues.

The remains of the Castle of Balsamão are located in a rural, isolated setting nearly at the summit of Mount Balsamão, formerly known as Monte Carrascal, which has an elevation of 522 meters. The castle is situated 4 kilometers from the seat of the parish. Constructed partially on rocky outcroppings, the castle adapts to the sloping terrain. The interior of the surviving, elevated fortification walls is covered with pine trees and vegetation. At the top of the hill stands the Convent of Balsamão, while along the slope are the eight chapels of the Stations of the Passion of Christ. Immediately near the wall are the Chapel of Cajados and the cemetery. The Azibo River, a tributary of the Sabor, flows to the south of the mount, crossed by the Paradinha Bridge, and the Ribeira de Veiga is located to the north.

The Melle Hills are home to several attractions, including the Diedrichsburg Wildlife Park in the Meller Mountains (approx. 28 km from Bielefeld). Founded in 1968, the park spans 35 hectares and is particularly popular with tourists in the autumn. It is known for its large wild boar population. On the Friedenshöhe, a subpeak of the Stuckenberg near Buer, stands an observation tower built in 1892, which has since been expanded into a restaurant. The Ottoshöhe, best reached from the "Waldstrasse" parking lot on the edge of Melle, also features a scenic viewpoint.

Melanie Oxley is the daughter of Eric and Jan Oxley. Eric, an art teacher originally from Manchester, migrated from Sydney to Kingscliff in 1965, where the family settled. Melanie has two older brothers, Peter and Jeremy Oxley, who co-founded the band Sunnyboys. She also has two younger brothers, Tim and Damien. Tim Oxley is a musician who has performed as a singer, guitarist, and drummer in various bands.

According to the 2000 census, Isle of Hope, Georgia, had a population of 2,106 people, with 1,000 households and 617 families residing in the area. The population density was 1,107 people per square mile, and there were 1,071 housing units, resulting in an average housing density of 558 units per square mile. In 2000, 96.8% of the population identified as White, while 0.5% were Hispanic or Latino of any race.

Kunitarō Sawamura came from a prominent acting family, with his younger siblings being actress Sadako Sawamura and actor Daisuke Katō. He married Tomoko Makino, the daughter of Shozo Makino, a pioneering film director known as the "father of Japanese cinema." Through this marriage, Kunitarō became the brother-in-law of directors Masahiro and Sadatsugu Makino. Masahiro Makino, known for jidaigeki and musicals, married post-war singing star Hibari Misora. Kunitarō and Tomoko had two sons, Hiroyuki Nagato and Masahiko Tsugawa, both of whom became acclaimed actors. Masahiko married actress Yukiji Asaoka. Kunitarō’s grandson, Masayuki Makino, founded the Okinawa Actors School, which trained notable performers like Namie Amuro, continuing the family’s legacy in the entertainment industry.

Bud Fowler began his professional baseball career in 1878 at the age of 20, playing for the all-white team, the Richburg Club. He is documented as playing for another professional team on July 21, 1877, when he was 19 years old. On April 24, 1877, Fowler pitched for a "picked nine," which defeated the Boston Red Stockings, the defending champions of the National League, in a 2-1 victory. The Boston Red Caps held the championship title that year.

Cormac McCarthy's novel *The Road* garnered significant recognition. In 2006, McCarthy was awarded the James Tait Black Memorial Prize in fiction and the Believer Book Award, and was a finalist for the National Book Critics Circle Award. On April 16, 2007, *The Road* won the Pulitzer Prize for fiction, and in 2012, it was shortlisted for the Best of the James Tait Black.

Jeremy Shepherd signed with the Brampton Lions for the 2008 CSL season, ending a two-year goal drought on September 20 with two goals in a 3-1 victory over the North York Astros. He later scored twice in a 6-0 win against London City, helping the Lions secure the seventh playoff spot. Re-signed on March 29, 2009, Shepherd contributed to Brampton finishing third in the National Division that season, earning another postseason berth.

Geles Cabrera's sculpture focuses on the human body and its geometry, exploring themes like love, sexuality, loneliness, and motherhood. Initially working with stone, she later incorporated materials like clay, bronze, and molded acrylic, using lyrical simplicity to emphasize the eroticism and clarity of the human form.

During the German Operation Nordwind offensive in January 1945, the Esch casemate came under fire as the US VI Corps and the German XXXIX Panzer Corps engaged in intense fighting in the Hatten area. The battle began on January 9 and continued until January 19, with the US 14th Armored Division suffering approximately 16,000 casualties. Civilians in Hatten also faced severe losses, with 83 killed during the conflict. The fighting caused significant damage to the casemate, which was transformed into a museum post-war. The area was eventually recaptured by the US forces on March 18, 1945, marking a pivotal moment in the campaign.

The construction and upgrading of the 136 km Isiolo–Merille road, part of the LAPSSET corridor, commenced in 2007 and was completed in 2011, transforming it into a two-lane tarmac road. The Marsabit–Turbi road construction began on 29 August 2011, with an expected delivery date of 4 April 2014.

On **2 July 2012**, the Syrian army killed at least **40 people** by evening, including **12 in Damascus suburbs**, **10 in Hama**, and **18 in Homs**. Meanwhile, **85 soldiers**, including a general and seven officers, defected to Turkey, with other accounts confirming the presence of a high-ranking defector.

Göreme's history spans thousands of years, serving as a living space for civilizations like the Hittites, Phrygians, Romans, Byzantines, Seljuks, and Ottomans. Initially a small settlement away from main roads, its isolation may have contributed to its role as a necropolis in Roman times, evidenced by pillared tombs hollowed into fairy chimneys. During the Byzantine era, the area consisted of two adjacent villages—Matiana and Corama (later Korama)—with the modern Open Air Museum located near this historic site. The first written record of these villages dates to the 6th century, referencing St. Hieron, a Christian martyr born in Korama whose severed hand was returned to his mother, as depicted in local churches. Simpler cave churches in the area date from as early as the 4th century, while their famed frescoes span the 9th to 13th centuries. The Council of Chalcedon (451), attended by Pope Leo I's legates and imperial officials, further highlights the region's Christian significance. Göreme later flourished as a monastic center before evolving into today's cultural and historical hub.

L. J. Figueroa began his high school basketball career at Notre Dame Cristo Rey High School in Lawrence, Massachusetts, before transferring to Lawrence High School for his sophomore season, where he became a standout player. Seeking greater opportunities, he moved to Oldsmar Christian School in Florida, where he experienced significant growth both physically and athletically, adding several inches to his height. Figueroa was brought to Oldsmar Christian by coach Alex Arias, who recognized his potential. For his senior year, he transferred to West Oaks Academy in Orlando, Florida, where he completed his high school career. Initially committing to New Mexico State out of high school, Figueroa later played collegiately at Odessa College and St. John’s.

Tri-Chandra Multiple Campus, originally named Tribhuvan Chandra Intermediate College, holds the distinction of being Nepal's first institution of higher education, established in 1918 by Chandra Shumsher Rana. A year later, the college introduced science at the Intermediate level, marking the beginning of formal science education in Nepal. In 1924, it was renamed Tri-Chandra College, and bachelor's level classes commenced. Initially, the college was affiliated with the University of Calcutta, later transitioning to Patna University. Until 1951 (2007 BS), access to the college was restricted to the elite members of the Rana regime. The primary purpose of science education at the time was to prepare students for technical studies in India. Science teaching was upgraded to the Bachelor level (B.Sc.) in 1945. In 1959, Tri-Chandra College was constituted into Tribhuvan University.

Captain Selwyn "Scully" Levin, born in Pietersburg, Transvaal (now Polokwane, Limpopo) in 1946, is a prominent member of the Flying Lions Aerobatic Team. He joined South African Airways in 1971 at the age of 25 and has accumulated over 27,000 hours of flight experience. After reaching the mandatory retirement age at SAA, he continued to fly professionally and leads the Gabriel Wings Aerobatic Team, showcasing his skills in various airshows.

The Walter Reed Army Institute of Research (WRAIR) dates back to 1893, when it was originally established as the Army Medical School (Context 8). Lieutenant Duff was assigned to the Army Medical School at the Walter Reed Army Medical Center (Context 5). The approaching war interrupted these studies and he became chief of the Division of Parasitology, and was placed in charge of a new course in Tropical Medicine (Context 5).

On September 11, 1842, General Adrian Woll led a force of 1,400 men to invade Texas and capture San Antonio. Robert Neighbors, serving in John C. Hays's company of volunteers, was attending court in San Antonio on September 15 when he and approximately 52 others, including court officers, were captured. They were forcibly marched to Perote Prison in Mexico, where Neighbors was held until his release on March 24, 1844.

Tannuella, a mollusk from the Early Cambrian (around 530 million years ago), is considered a possible precursor to cephalopods. Its chambered shell and structural features suggest a link to early cephalopod evolution. While the oldest confirmed cephalopod fossil is Plectronoceras, Tannuella is hypothesized to represent an ancestral form, potentially bridging the gap between monoplacophorans and cephalopods.

The Williams Rugby Football Club (WRFC) was founded in 1959 by David Coughlin after studying abroad in England. Supported by its first coach, Dr. Bruce Williams, who learned rugby in South Africa, the club entered the Eastern Rugby Union (ERU) that fall. In 1960, the WRFC won the ERU Championship, sharing the Carling Cup with Dartmouth, finishing with a 5-0-1 record. Increased support and recognition from the College came in 1961, when the team gained access to the Aldrich-Dexter Athletic Complex and adopted authentic rugby kits, solidifying its presence on campus.

In the early 1960s, Alan Douglas assisted Duke Ellington with errands in Paris while Ellington worked on the score for *Paris Blues*. After his time in Paris, Douglas joined United Artists and moved to New York, where Ellington made a surprise visit, suggesting they record a piano-focused album. Douglas proposed Charles Mingus as the bassist, while Mingus insisted on Max Roach as the drummer. Mingus had briefly deputized for Ellington’s regular bassist in 1953 but was fired after an on-stage altercation over a racial slur. This collaboration ultimately led to the creation of *Money Jungle*, a landmark jazz album featuring Ellington, Mingus, and Roach.

Floradale, Ontario, developed around Canagagigue Creek, where a flour mill, sawmill, flax mill, and cider mill supported the village and surrounding rural area. A school, post office (established in 1863), and various shops also served the community. Early churches included a Mennonite meetinghouse, a Lutheran church, and an Evangelical Association church, reflecting the religious diversity of the settlers. These institutions played a central role in the village's growth and the lives of its inhabitants.

The village of Tyra, first mentioned in 1577, was originally part of Oldřichovice, as described in records from 1580. It later grew into an independent settlement. Tyra belonged to the Duchy of Teschen, which was a fee of the Kingdom of Bohemia. When first documented, Tyra had approximately 400 inhabitants. Over time, it became a municipal district of Třinec in 1980, surrounded by the Beskydy Mountains and known for its natural springs and the Tyrka River.

**The Island** in Lake Billy Chinook is located between the Deschutes and Crooked river branches, spanning 208 acres (84 hectares) and measuring approximately 10 miles (16 km) in length. Surrounded on three sides by water, it features cliffs ranging from 390 to 702 feet (120–214 m) in height. Designated a Research Natural Area by the Bureau of Land Management (BLM) in 1948, it was closed to the public in 1931. Between May and mid-February, only researchers with BLM permits are permitted to visit.

Andros Townsend made his senior Tottenham debut on 9 January 2011 in an FA Cup third-round match at White Hart Lane against Charlton Athletic, scoring the opening goal in a 3–0 win. Following his debut, he joined Millwall on loan on 20 January 2011, scoring once in 11 appearances and expressing hope in April of extending his stay. Earlier in the 2010–11 season, he had a season-long loan at Ipswich Town terminated on 20 December 2010 and a subsequent loan at Watford, where he made three appearances before returning to Tottenham in January. These moves marked the beginning of a series of loans that characterized his early career.

*Stoner Witch*'s legacy is cemented by critical acclaim. *Spin* listed it as number 18 on its list of 'The 20 Greatest Grunge Albums of All Time'. Treblezine featured it as one of 'The 30 Best Grunge Albums' and '10 Essential Stoner Rock Albums'.

The Anthony Massad Three-Decker, located in Worcester, Massachusetts, is a historic three-story wood-frame structure. It features a hip roof and a front facade adorned with a three-tier porch supported by square posts. A polygonal bay projects from one side, adding architectural interest. Originally, the porch openings were simple and unadorned, reflecting the building's utilitarian design. This type of structure, common in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, was built to accommodate multiple families, often housing workers in the growing industrial city.
*The Hard Way* debuted in third place, earning $6.3 million. The films ahead are not specified in the provided context. The worldwide gross earnings are not specified in the provided context.

After going undrafted in the 2011 NFL Draft, Chas Henry signed with the Philadelphia Eagles as an undrafted free agent. He began the 2011 NFL season as the Eagles' starting punter. In Week 9 against the Chicago Bears on Monday Night Football, Henry botched a fake punt, underthrowing his pass in a critical blunder.

Dodji is a town situated in north-central Senegal, within the Louga Region. Few permanent settlements were established in this area until the early 20th century when it was founded by members of the Mouride brotherhood. These settlements aimed to cultivate previously uninhabited areas in eastern Senegal. In May 1963, a general strike called by the UNTS (the main workers' union center) led to the deportation of strike leaders to Dodji Camp in Ferlo, effectively decapitating the union. Before this, the area around Dodji was used as a remote deportation location and likely as a training ground by the French military.

Wm. Roger Louis is a renowned historian whose writings on the British Empire span the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, with a particular focus on the Middle East. His first major book, *Imperialism at Bay: The United States and the Decolonization of the British Empire, 1941–1945* (1977), examines the transformative years of World War II and the role of the United States in British decolonization. His second and most celebrated work, *The British Empire in the Middle East, 1945–1951: Arab Nationalism, the United States, and Postwar Imperialism* (1984), explores the interplay of British and American imperialism with Middle Eastern nationalism during the postwar years, including the policies of Clement Attlee’s cabinet. This book earned the George Louis Beer Prize and is considered a classic in the field.

Under the Federal Insurance Contributions Act (FICA), wages paid by a parent to their child under 18 are exempt from FICA taxes if the parent's business is a sole proprietorship, single-member LLC, or a partnership where both partners are the child's parents. The exemption does not apply to corporations (including S corporations) or partnerships with non-parent partners. For domestic service, the age limit is 21.

St Peter's College, Auckland, has a rich sporting tradition dating back to its early years. The college affiliated with the secondary schools' rugby union in 1939 and won the seventh grade rugby competition in 1941. The first annual College swimming championships were held on 21 March 1941 at the Olympic pool in Newmarket. Softball also became an integral part of the school's sports program, with students recorded as participating in competitions as early as 1945. St Peter's achieved significant success in softball, winning the inaugural national Secondary School's Softball Championship in 1981 and securing additional national titles in 1990, 1999, 2000, 2002, and 2003. The college dominated the Auckland Softball Premiership, winning every year from 1994 to 2008 and on several occasions since. Boxing was also a prominent sport in the early years, with annual school championships typically held at the Municipal Hall in Newmarket.

The William S. DeLano House, also known as Delano Farms, is designed in the Medieval saltbox style. The house has a fieldstone foundation and was constructed using post and beam construction. The main block of the house is two stories high. A porch with square supports fronts the wing. "The Marriage" has been the subject of extensive analysis in discussions about the artistic potential of video games, particularly within the framework of proceduralist design. Writers and scholars have taken interest in its minimalist approach, focusing on how its mechanics convey meaning without relying on traditional narrative or aesthetic elements. Ian Bogost categorized *The Marriage* as a proceduralist game, emphasizing that such games rely on "computational rules to produce their artistic meaning," rather than prioritizing visuals, sound, or text. Bogost compared *The Marriage* to other experimental games like Rod Humble's *A Love Story* and Stephen Lavelle's *Home*, highlighting its ability to express complex ideas through gameplay mechanics alone. Scholars have noted that the reductionist design of *The Marriage*—stripping away conventional storytelling and graphical detail—has sparked debate about the balance between rules and player interpretation in games. Theresa Claire Devine further explored this dynamic by developing a methodology for analyzing games as art, using *The Marriage* as a case study to demonstrate how games can function as expressive, interpretive works. Additionally, some writers have argued that *The Marriage* serves as a testament to the artistic potential of games, showcasing how interactive systems can evoke emotional and intellectual responses typically associated with more traditional art forms.

Polysomal Profiling is a technique that uses cycloheximide to arrest translation and a sucrose gradient to separate the resulting cell extract by centrifugation. Ribosome-associated mRNAs migrate faster than free mRNAs and polysome associated mRNAs migrate faster than ribosome associated mRNAs. Several peaks corresponding to mRNA are revealed by the measurement of total protein across the gradient. The corresponding mRNA is associated with increasing numbers of ribosomes as polysomes. The presence of mRNA across the gradient reveals the translation of the mRNA. Polysomal profiling is optimally applied to cultured cells and tissues to track the translational status of an identified mRNA as well as measure ribosome density. This technique has been used to compare the translational status of mRNAs in different cell types.

The difficulty of determining whether arson has occurred arises because fire often destroys the key evidence of its origin. Many fires are caused by defective equipment, such as shorting of faulty electrical circuits. Car fires can be caused by faulty fuel lines, and spontaneous combustion is possible where organic wastes are stored. A fire investigator looks at the fire remains, and obtains information to reconstruct the sequence of events leading up to the fire.

First single "Love Found Me (Love’s Got a Hold)" is the only obvious example of the band's Southern rock influences, sounding particularly like The Black Crowes with its underlying piano, horns and female backing vocals. In April 1993, Valacich et al. suggested that in light of new media, concurrency be included as an additional characteristic to determine a medium's richness. Furthermore, they explain that while this idea of concurrency could be applied to the media described in Daft and Lengel's original theories, new media provide a greater opportunity for concurrency than ever before.

When invading a colony, P. citrinus acquire the host scent as well as transfer it using mauling to camouflage and dominate the colony. Many species in the subgenus Psithyrus use scent to evade the host colony's recognition cues. Long chain alkanes and alkenes may possibly play a role in the production of Psithyrus’ scent. The Dufour's gland is enlarged in the Psithyrus subgenus and believed to be the source of chemical production for bees in the genus.

The calendar is a lunisolar calendar which adopted Teiki-hō, where solar terms are equally divided by solar longitude, in place of Heiki-hō, in which they are equally divided by time. The lunar month starts on a day with a new moon. A leap month is added when there are three lunar months between a lunar month which includes a solstice/equinox and the following lunar month which includes a solstice/equinox. In that case the leap month is the lunar month which does not include any chūki 中気 (one of the twelve solar terms that are used to determine the months of the year). The months which include a solstice/equinox are fixed as the second, fifth, eighth and eleventh months respectively. The time of the day used in the calendar to determine the dates of solar terms and lunar phases is that observed at Kyoto.

Each team plays 162 games per season. A team's schedule is typically organized into three-game series, sets of consecutive games against the same opponent, with occasional two- or four-game series. Postponed games or continuations of suspended games can result in an ad hoc one-game or five-game series. All games of a series are usually hosted by the same team and multiple series are typically grouped together. I.e, a team usually hosts several series in a row, called a homestand, and follows that by going on several road series in a row. Teams generally play games five to seven days per week. Most games are scheduled at night, although teams will often play day games on Opening Day, holidays, and for the last game of a series to allow teams extra time to travel to their next opponent. Sunday games are generally played during the afternoon, allowing teams to travel to their next destination prior to a Monday night game.

WU virus was discovered by shotgun sequencing of the nucleic acids found in the respiratory secretions of a young patient diagnosed with pneumonia in whom other known respiratory viruses had not been detected. The Aston Hall Hospital site displays evidence of a multi-phase prehistoric landscape which spans the Mesolithic through to the Late Iron Age; Sherds of undecorated, carinated bowl tradition pottery dating to the Early Neolithic, Grooved Ware of Clacton style (in use between 2900 cal BC and 2100 cal BC) and Flints dating to the Early Neolithic. Pits contained hazelnuts, mollusc shell, charcoal, spelt and rye grains. An East Midlands variant of the Deverel-Rimbury ceramic tradition, currently dated to the Middle Bronze Age were found to have similar fabrics containing rounded quartz sand and clastic sedimentary rock fragments as temper. Dating evidence from the [nearby] Aston Cursus, Willington Cursus, and associated earlier and later funerary barrows have normally been attributed to the Middle and Later Neolithic, however, the Early Neolithic pits across the site, although infrequent, demonstrate human activity in this landscape as early as the 4th millennium BC.

The first article about this species was published in 1875 by Edward Drinker Cope who wrote of the toad as a particular but unnamed variety of Bufo valliceps from eastern Costa Rica. It was described as a novel species in its own right in 1877 by Cope under the name B. melanochlorus. The holotype is a specimen (undesignated in Cope's original publication, but thought to be USNM30592) taken in Limón Province sometime before 1877 by W. W. Gabb. The specific epithet melanochlorus is compounded from the Ancient Greek μελανω (melanō), meaning "blackened" and χλωρός (khlōrós) meaning “(yellowish) green”. The English name invented by Frank and Ramus is thus a simple transliteration.

The white shell reaches a height of 1 mm. The solid shell has a depressed, turbinate shape. It is openly perforate to imperforate. The sculpture shows distant longitudinal lamellate ribs that cross the whorl from the suture to the umbilicus. Their interstices contain raised spiral threads, which grow coarser on approaching the umbilicus. The protoconch contains 11⁄2 whorl, concluding with a prominent varix. The three whorls are tabulate above, and rounded below. The body whorl descends rapidly. The aperture is roundly ovate. The sharp outer lip is convex. The inner lip spreads as a distinct callosity over the body, and sometimes seals the umbilicus partly or wholly up. The columella is concave. The narrow umbilicus is deep, and is bordered with a raised ridge, or is closed up. The foramen is large, and distant from the margin, to which a furrow joins it. The fasciole is extremity short, terminating half a whorl behind the aperture. it is bordered by keels and is traversed by lamellae, which correspond to the longitudinal ribs.

Alexandria, the ancient capital of Egypt, declined over the Mamluk period. The most notable Mamluk monument in the city is the Citadel of Qaytbay, built afterwards upon the ruins of the lighthouse. There are no official policies regarding human by-catch data among research and conservation organizations. Each organization may have its own policies. The technology is still emerging, but there are potential solutions. There are software programs, whose original intent is to ease the burden of the thousands of images that must be checked and logged during a camera trap study. The programs assist in automating the process of filtering out the false positives; photos that were triggered without the target species. Along with filtering out empty frames, this software can detect human presence, and filter out photos of humans. This way, privacy could be ensured if the process was automated, so that no person had access to the photos of the people that were inadvertently taken. Most of the government policies and laws regarding privacy with these technologies are aimed at drones.

Around 160–220 thousand years ago the summit of the Las Cañadas I volcano collapsed, creating the Las Cañadas (Ucanca) caldera. Later, a new stratovolcano, Las Cañadas II, formed in the vicinity of Guajara and then catastrophically collapsed. Another volcano, Las Cañadas III, formed in the Diego Hernandez sector of the caldera. All of the Las Cañadas volcanoes attained a maximum altitude similar to that of Teide (which is sometimes referred to as the Las Cañadas IV volcano).

The ear is particularly vulnerable to avulsion injuries because of its position on the side of the head. The most common cause of ear avulsions is human bites, followed by falls, motor vehicle collisions, and dog bites. A partially avulsed ear can be reattached through suturing or microvascular surgery, depending on the severity of the injury. Microvascular surgery can also be used to reattach a completely avulsed ear, but its success rate is lower because of the need for venous drainage. The ear can also be reconstructed with cartilage and skin grafts or an external ear prosthesis can be made by an anaplastologist.

Another mass execution was discovered near the eastern city of Deir ez-Zor. The unidentified corpses of 13 men had been discovered shot to death execution-style. This incident raised awareness that the violence in Syria was heading towards an inexorable vicious cycle of tit-for-tat attacks between the different parties involved. According to the opposition, the 13 people who were shot at point blank range and later found in a field were employees at the electricity company in Deir Ezzor, who went on strike in protest of the massacres committed by the Syrian regime. They all had their hands tied behind their backs and were shot in the head according to a UN report. The head of a UN observer mission in Syria was “deeply disturbed” by the killings in Deir Ezzor, calling it an “appalling and inexcusable act.” Several days later, the dead were identified to be military and the opposition than claimed they were army defectors killed by government forces. However, on 5 June, the jihadist group the Al-Nusra Front to Protect the Levant claimed responsibility for the killings, stating that they had captured and interrogated the soldiers in Deir al-Zor and "justly" punished them with death, after they confessed to crimes.

On 25 June 2008 a moulding company in Leicester was fined £5,300 and ordered to pay £2,134.10 after an employee suffered serious burns after he removed a mould plug during a routine operation at Harrison Castings Ltd. The burns required several skin grafts and five days in hospital for the employee. Inspector Munera Sidat said that the accident could have been prevented had the company provided the right type of gloves saying "Instead of foundry gloves which provide heat resistance, he was wearing rigger gloves which offered him very little protection. Not only did the molten metal permeate straight through the material, but the gloves were also so short that the liquid went up his jacket sleeves, making his burns worse." The company was charged under regulation 6 of the regulations which states that an assessment of the PPE provided should be made to ensure that it is suitable for the task".

The base of the building contains a steel superstructure with the Shops at Columbus Circle, Jazz at Lincoln Center, broadcast studios, and originally AOL Time Warner's headquarters. The towers' concrete superstructures rest above the base. Structurally, the building's base also includes the steel-framed lower sections of both towers. The steel frame extends 315 feet (96 m) high below the north tower and 349 feet (106 m) high below the south tower. Several column arrangements are used because of the differing needs of each tenant. Twenty-four entrances were originally provided at the base. To avoid interfering with the entrances and other open spaces, the building uses diagonal steel columns; concrete columns with stepped notches; and columns hanging from trusses.

A graduate of Polish Studies at the University of Warsaw. He made his debut as a poet in 1961 in the Nowa Kultura weekly. Open Skies aircraft may have video, optical panoramic, and framing cameras for daylight photography, infrared line scanners for a day/night capability, and synthetic aperture radar for a day/night all weather capability. O'Donnabhain v. Commissioner 134 T.C. No. 4 is a case decided by the United States Tax Court in 2010. The issue for the court was whether a taxpayer who has been diagnosed with gender identity disorder can deduct sex reassignment surgery costs as necessary medical expenses under 26 U.S.C. § 213. The Internal Revenue Service (IRS) argued that such surgery is cosmetic and not medically necessary. On February 2, 2010, the court ruled that O'Donnabhain should be allowed to deduct the costs of her treatment for gender-identity disorder, including sex-reassignment surgery and hormone treatments. In its decision, the court found the IRS position was "at best a superficial characterization of the circumstances" that is "thoroughly rebutted by the medical evidence".

Natalie Angier, writing in The New York Times, called The Change: Women, Ageing, and the Menopause (1991) a "brilliant, gutsy, exhilarating, exasperating fury of a book ... tantalizingly close to being a potential feminist classic on a par with The Female Eunuch." In it, Greer writes of the myths about menopause—or as she prefers to call it the "climacteric", or critical period. "Frightening females is fun", she wrote in The Age in 2002. "Women were frightened into using hormone replacement therapy by dire predictions of crumbling bones, heart disease, loss of libido, depression, despair, disease and death if they let nature take its course." She argues that scaring women is "big business and hugely profitable". The book, including the medical information, was updated and reissued in 2018.

Holbeach Clough is also known as Saracen's Head, with late 18th- and early 19th-century maps including both names. Saracen's Head might be associated with two public houses of that name that existed at the settlement—and still exist in modern form—one for perhaps 400 years, the name possibly taken from the coat of arms of significant local Willoughby, Irby, or Littlebury families. The Saracen's Head public house might have served travellers and traders crossing the then wide estuary of the River Welland to Fosdyke. The Anglo-Saxon coastline of the area ran immediately north of Holbeach Clough until a dike, the Roman Bank, was constructed for land reclamation in the 14th century, this running from the west at Moulton Seas End. The Saracen's Head Ward of the local council covers Holbeach Clough.

In 1984, they founded GoodTimes Home Video Corporation (eventually renamed GoodTimes Entertainment) with 25 Public Domain video titles. They expanded into the production and distribution of low-priced fitness videos although its most recognized line of products were a series of low-budget traditionally animated films. Many of its home-video titles (such as Aladdin, Beauty and the Beast, Jungle Book, Hercules, Pocahontas, Cinderella, The Little Mermaid, Sinbad, and Thumbelina) were named similarly or identically to big-budget animated films from other studios (though their plots were sometimes very different), and released close to the theatrical or home-video releases of the other studios. Walt Disney Pictures sued GoodTimes because the packaging of the GoodTimes videotapes closely resembled that of Disney potentially confusing customers into buying a GoodTimes movie when they thought they were buying a Disney movie. As a result of that lawsuit, GoodTimes was required to print its name atop its VHS covers, but it was still allowed to produce animated films.

Film Threat praised Shepard for his portrayal of "Steven Cozza as just a normal kid who had the good fortune to be raised by a couple of great parents...it's unfathomable that a young man could take on such a sensitive cause, in a truly insensitive age bracket...and be accepted for it". Eastern Kentucky native, Dr. Patrick Angel initially spent most of his career within the Office of Surface Mining Reclamation and Enforcement instructing mining companies on how to fix land that was negatively affected by strip-mining and mountaintop removal mining. These efforts consisted of packing soil tightly together and planting grass, a plant which Angel says was the "only type of plant he trusted to hold the ground in place". In 2002, scientists like Dr. Angel started realizing that Appalachian forests destroyed by strip-mining weren't coming back, even with rejuvenation efforts. In Appalachia, a land mass larger than the state of Delaware should have been covered in trees, yet only held grass. After realizing this was an ecological disaster, Dr. Angel led efforts to plant more than 187 million trees which cover more than 275,000 acres of former mining land in Appalachia. Dr. Kathy Newfont, an Appalachian history professor at the University of Kentucky commented on Dr. Angel's efforts saying "...it was one of the most hopeful things I'd heard about the region in decades,".

By the mid-1950s Gibson Guitar Company had lost considerable market share to rival Fender's Telecaster and Stratocaster models and created three modernistic solid-body guitar concepts in an attempt to strike back. On June 27, 1957, Gibson President Ted McCarty filed three designs with the United States Patent Office which would come to be called "Flying V", "Explorer", and (unofficially, by later historians) "Moderne". The design patents were issued on January 7, 1958; however, after getting an exceedingly poor reception at NAMM 1957, Gibson decided not to produce the Moderne. There is only a vague mention of "Mod. Guitars" on a shipping list, which some argue applies to Explorers. Gibson engineer Rendall Wall (and later co-founder of Heritage Guitars) played it in the early 1960s for a local performance of Bye Bye Birdie. Wall recounted that he got it out of Gibson's "morgue", and that he could have bought it for $50: it was so ugly that nobody wanted it. He returned it after the show, saying he was more a country player than a rock and roll player.

The M777 began in 1987 as the Ultralight Field Howitzer (UFH), developed by Vickers Shipbuilding and Engineering's (VSEL) Armaments Division in Barrow-in-Furness, UK. VSEL was bought by BAE Systems after the UFH prototypes had been manufactured and demonstrated, and consequently BAE became responsible for future design refinements and renamed the gun "M777". Upon taking over responsibility for the weapon, BAE "Americanised" to a large degree the construction and assembly through its US-based BAE Systems Land and Armaments group. The M777 now uses about 70% US-built parts including the gun barrel, which is manufactured at the Watervliet Arsenal.

During character creation the player chooses a class: fighter, ranger, paladin, thief, bard, mage, cleric, druid, barbarian, monk, or sorcerer (the last three of which are new for the game). Different classes have different special abilities and restrictions; a thief character, for instance, can find and remove traps, but thieves have limitations on which weapons and armor they may use, and cannot be of lawful good alignment. Most classes also have a subset of kits, or specializations within a class, to choose from. Kits have special advantages and, usually, disadvantages; for example, the cavalier—one of the kits of the paladin class—specializes in fighting monsters such as dragons and demons, but cannot use missile weapons. At some point in the game, the player may join or take over a stronghold. The type of stronghold is determined by the protagonist's class.

The theory of constructed emotion (TCE) offers an explanation of the basis of emotion by formulating the brain as a running internal model that controls central pattern generators in order to maintain allostasis. This theory argues that the computational goal of the brain is to minimize the prediction error, unpredicted events, that arise in a particular sensory environment. Once prediction error is minimized, the brain's predictions become experiences and perceptions and the brain categorizes sensory events. In this manner, the brain continually updates and constructs its categorizations and predictions. When the brain's internal model constructs an emotion concept, the subsequent categorization elicits an emotion. It is hypothesized that the brain's default mode network is necessary to the generation its internal model while the salience network tunes the internal model by minimizing prediction error.

Conservation for National Museum of the American Indian's collection is conducted at the Cultural Resources Center (CRC), the second of three facilities comprising the Smithsonian's National Museum of the American Indian. The facility is state-of-the-art and includes separate laboratories for working on textiles and objects, a technical library, a photography studio, a scientific analytical lab, and mount-making spaces. In addition, the office actively engages in conservation training—on and off-site— and is committed to conducting research working in partnership with Native Americans on ethnographic and archaeological materials.

The Royal Botanic Garden consist of 29 hectares (72 acres) of closely cultivated land surrounded by 51 hectares (130 acres) of open parklands comprising The Sydney Domain. The Garden forms a large natural amphitheatre, wrapped around and sloping down towards the 'stage' of Farm Cove. It is divided into four major precincts called the Lower Gardens, the Middle Gardens, the Palace Gardens and the Bennelong precinct. Within the four major precincts are many smaller gardens and features as well as large amounts of lightly wooded lawn areas. Located approximately in the middle of the four precincts is the Palm Grove Centre which offers a restaurant, cafe, visitors centre and bookshop. A large and complex public botanic garden, largely of late 19th-century character; being not only an historic site of the first importance but containing within it numerous structures which have been nominated separately by the National Trust of Australia (NSW). A predominantly nineteenth century character of landscape layout strengthened by large mature trees. Traditionally designated as four areas reflecting its development.

The rivalry was first played in 1890 on Minnesota's campus, in Minneapolis, resulting in a 63–0 Minnesota victory. Theron Lyman led Wisconsin to its first win over Minnesota in 1894. The game became a conference rivalry with the creation of the Western Conference (later the Big Ten Conference) in 1896. In 1906, President Theodore Roosevelt suspended college football rivalry games for safety concerns, due to player injuries and fatalities on the field. It is the single year the two teams did not play each other. Subsequently, it is now the longest uninterrupted rivalry in FBS Division 1 college football. The game has never been played in any city besides Minneapolis, and Madison, Wisconsin.

In the game's backstory, an event called the Rise of Morastrum occurs once every three centuries; a solar eclipse occurs, with every newborn of that year except one destined to die; this survivor is called the Child of Destiny and receives great power. 600 years prior, the Child of Destiny became an evil ruler called the Archfiend, while 300 years prior the Child of Destiny was a good hero called the Matriarch who toppled the Archfiend. Prior to the game's opening, the Rise of Morastrum occurs again, and people begin seeking the Child of Destiny to discover their eventual fate. The eight main characters, all known to each other, set out on their own quests and end up involved in the hunt for the Child of Destiny. They are Julian Nohl, a reckless and just swordsman; the tomboyish Ellen Carson; the shy Sarah Carson; Thomas Bent, friend to Julian, Ellen and Sarah; Monika Ausbach, sister to the Marquis of Loanne; Monika's brother Mikhail Ausbach von Loanne; Katarina Lauran, Monika's attendant; and Khalid, a wandering mercenary. They all cross paths in the village of Sinon.
